did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep neural architectures and supervised deep-learning algorithms for the classification of enhanced melanoma skin lesions. Specifically, it employs wavelet decomposition to extract entropy and statistical features, which are then classified using deep learning methods to achieve a classification accuracy of 93.6%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving image enhancement, wavelet decomposition, feature extraction, and classification using deep neural architectures and supervised learning algorithms. It reports specific results, including a classification accuracy of 93.6%, and compares its proposed method against other techniques using a publicly available dataset, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Wavelet-Based Classification of Enhanced Melanoma Skin Lesions through Deep Neural Architectures
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of melanoma skin lesions by focusing on frequency domain features rather than traditional spatial domain methods. The proposed computer-aided diagnosis system utilizes enhanced median filtering for image enhancement and Normalized Otsu’s segmentation for lesion isolation. Wavelet decomposition using DB4, Symlet, and RBIO functions is employed to extract seven entropy and eight statistical features from the segmented images. These features are classified using supervised and deep-learning algorithms on a publicly available dataset of skin lesion images. The system achieved a classification accuracy of 93.6%, which is 0.71% higher than spatial domain-based classification methods. The results suggest that this wavelet-based approach can assist clinicians in identifying skin cancer at earlier stages.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary focus of the study is the classification of melanoma skin lesions. The title and abstract explicitly state 'Classification of Enhanced Melanoma Skin Lesions'. The introduction discusses melanoma statistics and diagnosis. The dataset used contains images of melanoma lesions.

The paper mentions classifying 'melanoma and non-melanoma' images in the context of related works (e.g., reference [4], [6], [7], [8]). The abstract states the CAD system predicts whether a lesion is 'benign or malignant'. Reference [8] specifically mentions classifying 'melanoma and nevus lesions'. While the current study focuses on melanoma, the binary classification task implies the presence of non-melanoma/benign classes in the dataset or as the negative class.

all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on the classification of enhanced melanoma skin lesions. The system predicts whether a lesion is 'benign or malignant' (binary classification). The abstract and introduction explicitly state the goal is to classify melanoma and non-melanoma images. The results report classification accuracy (93.6%), sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1 score, which are metrics for classification tasks. While SVM is mentioned as capable of regression, the paper applies it and other algorithms (DLNN, ANN, ANFIS, Adaboost) specifically for classifying the extracted features into diagnostic categories.

datasets_used.name: Publicly available skin lesion dataset (referenced as [40] and [47])
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of skin lesion images used to implement and test the proposed computer-aided diagnosis system. The Data Availability Statement specifies that the images were acquired from repositories mentioned in reference [47] (Hosny et al., 2019, PLoS ONE). The dataset contains images with artifacts such as hair lines and ruler markers.

use.training: Used to train the classification algorithms (DLNN, SVM, ANN, ANFIS, Adaboost) after preprocessing (enhanced median filtering) and segmentation (Normalized Otsu’s).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the classification performance, achieving 93.6% accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Quality metric to assess the effectiveness of image enhancement (higher values indicate better quality).
Quality metric to compare true pixels of the original image to the degraded image (lower values indicate better quality).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1 score, Kappa, PSNR, and MSE, with comparisons made based on metric values rather than statistical significance testing.

Publicly available skin lesion dataset [.metric: Classification Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Kappa, Precision, F1 Score, Training Time, Testing Time
Publicly available skin lesion dataset [.notes: The proposed system achieved a classification accuracy of 93.6% (specifically 93.62% for DLNN with Symlet wavelet), which is 0.71% higher than spatial domain-based classification methods. The DLNN with Symlet function yielded the highest classification accuracy among all tested algorithms. Entropy-based features yielded higher classification accuracy than mean and variance of wavelet coefficients. A subtle difference of 0.07% was observed between spatial and frequency domain classification accuracy. Classification accuracy was interrupted for some samples due to overwhelming artifacts (hair lines and ruler markers).

architecture_used.main_model: Deep Learning Neural Network (DLNN), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS), and Adaboost variants (Real, Modest, Gentle, Hybrid). The best performing model was DLNN with Symlet wavelet features.
architecture_used.input_size: 15 input features (7 entropy features and 8 statistical features extracted from wavelet coefficients) and 2 output features.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Wavelet decomposition using DB4, Symlet, and RBIO functions to extract 7 entropy features (ApEn, SamEn, ShEn, LogEn, ThEn, SrNm, NmEn) and 8 statistical features (mean, median, Std_dev, Med_Abs_dev, Mean_Abs_dev, L1_Norm, L2_Norm, Range).
architecture_details.dlnn_structure: Not explicitly detailed in terms of layer count or neurons, but identified as the top-performing classifier.
architecture_details.anfis_structure: Triangular membership functions with 3 linguistic levels (low, medium, high) resulting in 315 rules for each wavelet function.

training_details.hyperparameters: Polynomial kernel for SVM; 315 rules for ANFIS.

preprocessing_techniques: Enhanced median filtering using three add-on techniques: Range method, Fuzzy Relational method, and Similarity coefficient method
Normalized Otsu’s segmentation for lesion isolation
conclusions: The proposed computer-aided diagnosis system, which utilizes enhanced median filtering, Normalized Otsu’s segmentation, and wavelet-based feature extraction, achieved a classification accuracy of 93.6%.
The wavelet-based approach yielded a classification accuracy 0.71% higher than traditional spatial domain-based classification methods.

Among the tested algorithms, the Deep Learning Neural Network (DLNN) combined with Symlet wavelet features demonstrated the highest performance, achieving 93.62% accuracy, 94.59% sensitivity, and 92.45% specificity.
Entropy-based features extracted from wavelet decomposition resulted in higher classification accuracy compared to statistical features derived from the mean and variance of wavelet coefficients.

The enhanced median filtering method based on the range method provided superior image quality (higher PSNR and lower MSE) compared to traditional median filtering and other proposed methods (Fuzzy Relational and Similarity Coefficient).
The study acknowledges that classification accuracy was interrupted for some samples due to overwhelming artifacts such as hair lines and ruler markers present in the dataset.
The proposed system has the potential to assist clinicians and dermatology specialists in identifying skin cancer at earlier stages.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a significant limitation where classification accuracy was interrupted for samples with overwhelming artifacts, specifically hair lines and ruler markers, which caused features to be randomly scattered and led to misclassification (Figure 6).

The dataset used is publicly available but acquired from repositories referenced in [47] (Hosny et al., 2019); specific details on dataset size, class distribution, or split ratios are not reported in this paper.

The performance gain of the proposed wavelet-based method over spatial domain methods is marginal, with a reported difference of only 0.71% in accuracy and a subtle difference of 0.07% between spatial and frequency domain classification accuracy.
The paper notes an 'overhead of high computational complexity' associated with the frequency domain approach compared to spatial domain methods.
No external validation was performed; the model was tested solely on the single publicly available dataset mentioned.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the significance of the performance differences between models.
The authors declare no conflict of interest and did not disclose specific funding for this work, though they acknowledge infrastructure support from the Department of Science and Technology, India.
